NDArray API

So, may be should add more comments. 
 Basically , ONNX pooling operator can have uneven padding,  as it specifies padding values at each dimension 
(axis_0_start, axis_1_start, axis_0_end, axis_1_end)  -- > (0,1,0,2) 

But Mxnet only supports even padding specified as (axis_0_start/end, axis_1_start/end) ---- >(0,1)  

To solve this issue , we follow the steps:
1. Apply padding on the tensor first , as Mxnet padding also supports uneven padding.
2. Once padding is done apply pooling without  any padding.

```
+                    api_response = client.delete_namespaced_pod(
+                        self._kubernetes_driver_pod,
+                        "default",
```
What I read from the docs, it isn't optional, so I'm not sure if `None` would work.:
https://github.com/kubernetes-client/python/blob/master/kubernetes/docs/CoreV1Api.md#delete_namespaced_pod

The suggestion that I had was to do this in a single line, with a default value, instead of the if-else construction.

I strongly believe starting (and deleting) a job using a configurable namespace should be in there. If you are using a single kubernetes cluster for one person/team, only using the default namespace would be ok. But having namespaces in the connection strings, allows us to kubernetes cluster among different teams/projects very nicely with the configured resources quota's.

We need to supply this when supply this to Spark when starting the job: https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/running-on-kubernetes.html#namespaces
And supply the same namespace when deleting the pod, otherwise Kubernetes cannot find the resource to delete.

    zeo-address = ${ZRS-Master} ${ZRS-Replica1} ... ${ZRS-ReplicaN} 

All ZRS-Replica are read-only by default, but ZRS-Master is writeable. 
ZRS-Master goes down, e.g. OS reboot, and ZRS-Replica takes over, at this moment the runtime config switches from read_only_mode = False to read_only_mode = True and back.

This constant is used in a special tracking header
- this constant is deliberately
  * specified by the code generator and 
  * is not related to the package
- its purpose is to track the version of the code-generator that was used to generate the code, *not* the name and version of the package the generated code is used in
- note that there is application name and application version that can be specified the service constructor instances; it's there that the actual package version is intended to be used

That said, regarding the justification you give for `FacilityDataset` I notice that two of the reasons have nothing to do with Morango, and one does. I caution against creating a class that has unrelated responsibilities. There *is* a concrete justification for this class arising purely from within this module, as you point out -- what I'm suggesting is to stick close to those reasons.

For instance, does it make sense for a `FacilityDataset` to exist without a `Collection` of kind "facility"? Should `FacilityDataset` be the "privileged" model, or should `Collection`s with kind "facility"? By "privileged" I mean should it be the foreign-key for objects that are namespaced by facilities. Sadly, without a more in-depth consideration that we don't seem to have the time for, I can only appeal to my intuition. (My intuition answers "no" and "`Collection`s", FWIW.)

As far as the implementation, what you're describing sounds very much like a [one-to-one relationship](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.9/topics/db/examples/one_to_one/), so you might consider if that's appropriate here.

The ``enum`` module was added in Python 3.4.  For Python 2.7, we either need to not use ``enum`` or add a conditional dependency on the ``enum34`` backport like so:

```Python
try:
    from enum34 import Enum
except ImportError:
    from enum import Enum
```

As I said in other comments here, I think it's a little awkward you can set `--apache-bin` but it may never be used, but it's not so bad. Unless there's a good reason to switch to the Apache binary that I haven't heard yet, I think I'd like us to:

1. Switch back to using `ctl` here.
2. Don't check for the path to `bin` unless it's going to be used.

I think we probably want to recommend `--post-hook` here. There are a couple differences:

1. `--renew-hook` will be run once for each cert is renewed. This means if `certbot renew` renews five certificates in one run, the `--renew-hook` is run five times as well.
2. `--renew-hook` is also saved for future renewals. If you renew a cert using `certbot renew --renew-hook "service nginx reload"`, future renewals can just use `certbot renew` and "service nginx reload" will be run whenever that cert is renewed.

yes -- the pypi-public nexus repository is specifically for generally available packages. It contains everything that team.wildcardcorp.com/eggs contained, except it is now managed by different software.

the repository can be browsed here: https://nexus.wildcardcorp.com/#browse/browse:pypi-public.

The reason for the direct links to packages is due to the hosting service generating package paths that buildout does not recognize properly when overriding packages that may already be on the index being used (pypi.org/simple).

I do think that being more explicit about the package locations helps to be clear about what customized packages are still used by castle currently.
